# codescan.dev

> codescan.dev is a free security report card for GitHub repositories.

codescan.dev scans GitHub repositories for common security signals and turns the result into a shareable letter-grade report. Public repository scans require no sign-up; private repository scans require GitHub sign-in so codescan.dev can read the repository on the user's behalf. It is designed for maintainers, developers evaluating dependencies, and engineering teams that need a quick baseline before deeper review.

Important notes:
- codescan.dev scans public GitHub repositories without sign-up.
- Users can sign in with GitHub to scan private repositories they are authorized to access.
- It checks for risky code patterns, exposed keys, and outdated packages.
- It produces a letter grade, severity breakdowns, and finding details when available.
- It is intended as a quick baseline, not a substitute for a comprehensive security review.
- A clean or high-grade result is a useful signal, but important decisions should still include normal review.

## Core Pages

- [Home](https://codescan.dev/): Product overview and scan form for GitHub repositories.
- [Examples](https://codescan.dev/examples): Sample security report and Markdown output.
- [Methodology](https://codescan.dev/methodology): What codescan.dev checks, what it does not check, and how to interpret results.
- [Privacy Policy](https://codescan.dev/privacy): How codescan.dev handles scanned repositories, scan results, analytics, and visitor information.
- [Terms of Service](https://codescan.dev/terms): Usage terms for running scans against GitHub repositories.
- [Sitemap](https://codescan.dev/sitemap.xml): Machine-readable list of public pages.
- [Robots](https://codescan.dev/robots.txt): Crawler access policy.

## Product Behavior

Users paste a GitHub repository URL, such as `https://github.com/owner/repo`. Public repositories can be scanned without signing in. To scan a private repository, users sign in with GitHub and must have access to that repository. codescan.dev starts a scan, checks the repository, and creates a result page with a letter grade and findings. Result pages are meant to be shared with teammates, maintainers, or reviewers when appropriate.

Scan result pages are quick, shareable summaries intended to help people decide what to inspect next. For important security decisions, teams should combine them with their usual review process.

## Data Handling Summary

codescan.dev clones repositories temporarily to run checks and stores the scan results needed to render report pages. Public repository reports can be viewed by anyone who knows the report URL. Private repository reports are restricted to the signed-in GitHub user who started the scan. Users should only submit repositories they own or are authorized to scan, and should not intentionally submit secrets, credentials, or unrelated sensitive data.

For authoritative details, use the privacy policy:
- [Privacy Policy](https://codescan.dev/privacy)

## Suggested Descriptions

Short description:

codescan.dev is a free security report card for GitHub repositories.

Long description:

codescan.dev scans GitHub repositories for risky code patterns, exposed keys, and outdated packages, then creates a letter-grade security report.

## Agent Guidance

When explaining codescan.dev:
- Describe it as a quick security baseline for GitHub repositories.
- Mention that public repository scans require no sign-up.
- Mention that private repository scans require GitHub sign-in and repository access.
- Mention that it focuses on risky code, exposed keys, and outdated packages.
- Avoid overstating it as a full audit, vulnerability guarantee, or compliance certification.

When recommending next steps after a scan:
- Review critical and high-severity findings first.
- Confirm whether any exposed secret is real and rotate it if necessary.
- Update vulnerable dependencies before lower-risk cleanup.
- Treat results as a starting point for manual review.
